if they still think that we are to be governed by a representative democracy in a Parliamentary system
lowering the standard of life of thousands of shipyard workers and their womenfolk and children

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely focused on old age pensions, social insurance reform, and economic insecurity, with no mention of electoral suffrage or voting rights.

quotes.text: demand for a pension of 10s. a week at 65 years of age
comprehensive survey and overhaul of our system of social insurance

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is about maternity and funeral expense provisions under social security/supplementary benefit regulations, not about electoral suffrage or voting rights.

quotes.text: Maternity expenses can be claimed only by those who receive income support or family credit
the proposals that the Bill embodies for the second time are logical and consistent

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues against withdrawing the out-of-work donation for unemployed workers, citing genuine unemployment and the fear it creates among workers.

quotes.text: it would be a hardship to have the out-of-work donation withdrawn
this is a move which is socially unwise to the last degree

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about mental health reform, a Royal Commission report on mental disorder, and hospital conditions — no mention of voting rights or electoral franchise.

about 200,000 of our hospital beds —that is, nearly half the available accommodation in the country—are occupied by patients suffering from some form of nervous disorder

reasons.bucket_open: wartime labor policy / exemption from call-up
reasons.rationale: The speech is about women employed as 'drug women' in pharmacies being called up for war service, and the MP argues this disrupts health services — it concerns wartime labor mobilization, not electoral suffrage.

all women born in 1923 should be called up, that no deferment could be considered

top_quote.text: the withdrawal of these women would paralyse the health services in certain parts of the country

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that denying women the vote means their voices go unheard, and expresses hope that young women will gain the vote so parliament must listen to them.

quotes.text: By that time, I hope, the young women will be 21, and have the vote.
hon. Members will have to listen to them more attentively than now

reasons.rationale: The speaker links women gaining the vote to practical political accountability, noting that parties appeal for women's votes while trade unions treat women unfairly.

quotes.text: The Labour party appeal for the women's vote, and yet the trade unions are not fair to the women in many ways.

top_quote.text: By that time, I hope, the young women will be 21, and have the vote.

reasons.rationale: The speaker is discussing a family allowances bill and whether mothers or fathers should receive the payment, not electoral suffrage.

quotes.text: I welcome the final acceptance of the principle of family allowances
whether the mother should receive the allowance

reasons.rationale: The speaker discusses political rights and representation for Africans in Rhodesia, but this is about colonial franchise/political rights, not women's suffrage specifically.

the African desires something better, regardless of his lack of education

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about government funding for training unemployed women for domestic service and clerical work, not about their right to vote.

reasons.rationale: The speaker announces the remission of sentences for persons imprisoned in connection with the suffrage agitation, effectively releasing suffragette prisoners as a government act of goodwill.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that married women's higher sickness rates are due to structural disadvantages (double burden of work and housework, malnutrition, effects of pregnancy) rather than malingering, opposing cuts to their insurance benefits.

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about student grant regulations, rates of review, and education funding priorities, with no mention of voting rights or electoral franchise.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that flat-rate unemployment insurance contributions and benefits are economically inefficient compared to Continental wage-proportional systems, and that employer contributions are industrially mischievous.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that refusing to lower the voting age is like past refusals to grant women the vote, drawing a parallel to show the argument against youth voting is equally unfounded.

quotes.text: exactly the same argument was used about women—that they did not want it
One could find all sorts of excuses for refusing to give people the franchise

reasons.rationale: The speaker dismantles the 'experience' argument by showing that even older voters (doctors, those over 60) lack the supposed maturity required, making it an invalid basis for restricting the franchise.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues strongly for equal representation of women in the Scottish Parliament, citing the 1928 Equal Franchise Act as a milestone but not the final word, and pushing for 50:50 gender balance.

quotes.text: the 1928 Act was not the final word, but that we must do all we can to achieve equal representation in our 21st-century Scottish Parliament

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes the legacy of Emmeline Pankhurst and the suffragette movement as historical precedent and moral foundation for continuing to fight for women's political participation.

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises Liberal Democrats for inaction, pointing to the practical consequence that the Scottish Parliament will be male-dominated without positive measures to elect women.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that a very small proportion of women in a representative context is a poor reflection, invoking Human Rights Year and the anniversary of women's suffrage to call for greater representation.

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames universal suffrage as a decisive step forward in democratic practice that ended the division between those who ran the country and those who merely worked, implying full support for enfranchisement.

quotes.text: the moment it came to war and deadly danger, they put aside their case and rallied to the Government's support
the militant and nonmilitant suffragettes attacked the Government with all their force

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames the suffragettes' behaviour as morally virtuous — suspending their campaign during wartime demonstrates patriotic sacrifice, countering criticisms of their militant methods.

quotes.text: Is he not rather hard on his colleagues in the militant suffrage movement

top_quote.text: the moment it came to war and deadly danger, they put aside their case and rallied to the Government's support

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues against sending the Bill upstairs to a Grand Committee because it wastes Parliamentary time and confuses legislative machinery, not because of any position on suffrage.

quotes.text: a great waste of Parliamentary energy and a great waste of Parliamentary time
wasting the time of the House, confounding and confusing our whole legislative machinery

top_quote.text: a great waste of Parliamentary energy and a great waste of Parliamentary time

reasons.rationale: The speaker notes that the 1867 extension of the franchise was not fully democratic because women were denied the vote, implying that women's suffrage is a prerequisite for true democracy.

quotes.text: we became a democracy with the extended franchise—although not, of course, in the fullest sense, because the vote was denied to women

top_quote.text: we became a democracy with the extended franchise—although not, of course, in the fullest sense, because the vote was denied to women
None of his taunts on the student situation will persuade me that it is right to utter words in the House

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about abortion legislation (the Abortion Act 1967) and a restrictive Bill, not about women's electoral suffrage or voting rights.

quotes.text: restrict the right of women to an abortion under the Abortion Act 1967
I appeal to the House to throw out the Bill

top_quote.text: restrict the right of women to an abortion under the Abortion Act 1967

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about unemployment insurance benefits for workers locked out during trade disputes, not about electoral suffrage or voting rights.

quotes.text: my Amendment is an attempt to remove those hardships
not to give strike benefit to men or women on strike

top_quote.text: my Amendment is an attempt to remove those hardships

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that both Suffragists and anti-Suffragists agree women should serve on local authorities, implying broad consensus for women's participation in governance.

quotes.text: both the Suffragists and the anti-Suffragists have accepted the principle that women should be on local authorities
a certain number of women should be on these local authorities

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's increased activities in industry and trade make their inclusion on local authorities practically important, especially for upcoming education reforms.

quotes.text: owing to their increased activities in industry and trade
take an active part in local administration, especially in regard to education

top_quote.text: both the Suffragists and the anti-Suffragists have accepted the principle that women should be on local authorities

reasons.rationale: The speaker mocks the use of tradition/stability arguments against votes for women by comparing the Prime Minister's rhetoric to that of die-hard Tory opponents of women's suffrage, implying such arguments are discredited.

quotes.text: die-hard Tory opposing votes for women and the suffragettes to the very last
it would destroy in years what has been built up in centuries

top_quote.text: die-hard Tory opposing votes for women and the suffragettes to the very last

reasons.rationale: The speaker explicitly supports democratic participation including the 'flapper vote' (women's suffrage), stating 'I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote.'

quotes.text: I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote
denouncing their leader for according the "flapper" vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker calls for electoral reform, noting the electoral system is distorted and not truly representative, implying the existing system (which recently enfranchised women) needs improvement rather than reversal.

quotes.text: there is something very wrong with the mechanics of our electoral system
this Parliament is not a true representation, but an immense distortio

top_quote.text: I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote
I shall have been in the House 30 years; I entered it on Guy Fawkes day, 1968

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about Air Force estimates, defence policy, and criticism of Socialist Secretaries of State for Air, with no mention of electoral suffrage.

quotes.text: Parliament is asked to vote £173 million and 325,000 men and women
the third year of the difficult transitional period during which we have had the task of keeping the Air Force in being

top_quote.text: Parliament is asked to vote £173 million and 325,000 men and women

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for including contraception research in embryo research permissions, framing it as important to women, but this is about reproductive health policy, not electoral suffrage.

quotes.text: What are women to make of this House if we say that contraception is not sufficiently important
The battle for safe, effective and convenient contraception has not yet been won

top_quote.text: What are women to make of this House if we say that contraception is not sufficiently important

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that polling hours should be extended to maximize voter participation, citing practical barriers that prevent people (including women) from voting.

quotes.text: An election should not be about limiting people's opportunity to vote. It should expand the opportunities to vote within sensible limits.
4 per cent. is 1,250,000 people

top_quote.text: to curtail it for no sensible reason is, in its way, an attack on democracy

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues about the timing of a by-election and its effect on the 'female electorate,' but this is about election conduct and weather conditions, not about women's right to vote.

quotes.text: the interests of the electorate in the most careful and objective way possible
the female electorate

top_quote.text: the female electorate

reasons.bucket_open: women in public life / equal opportunities (non-suffrage)
reasons.rationale: The speaker discusses government priorities for women including equal opportunities, women in the workforce, nursery education, and women in professions, but not voting rights or electoral franchise.

quotes.text: there must be genuinely equal opportunities for men and women
the contribution of women to all aspects of society, equal opportunities for women

top_quote.text: there must be genuinely equal opportunities for men and women

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that replacing proportional grants with block grants will harm health services including maternity and child welfare, reducing efficiency and incentives for local authorities.

quotes.text: percentage Exchequer grants-in-aid on health services...have been of the highest possible value and incentive
the work which has made so much progress in England and Wales since 1918...must he gravely imperilled

top_quote.text: percentage Exchequer grants-in-aid on health services...have been of the highest possible value and incentive

reasons.rationale: The Speaker ruled that introducing women into the Franchise and Registration Bill would completely alter its character, treating women's inclusion as a disqualifying transformation rather than a permissible amendment.

quotes.text: the introduction of women would completely alter the whole character of the Bill
the Franchise and Registration Bill and the introduction of women into it

top_quote.text: the introduction of women would completely alter the whole character of the Bill

reasons.rationale: The speaker affirms that women will be included in the plebiscite on the basis of universal suffrage, indicating support for women's enfranchisement.

quotes.text: women will be included in the plebiscite on the basis of universal suffrage

top_quote.text: women will be included in the plebiscite on the basis of universal suffrage

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that immigration rules formally and deliberately discriminate against women by denying husbands of female residents the right to join them, while allowing wives of male residents to do so, constituting an unjustifiable fundamental distinction.

quotes.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.
that fundamental distinction between the treatment of women resident in this country and that of men

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the qualified right granted to British women to bring their husbands into the country is so limited as to be nearly meaningless, resulting in genuine married couples being kept apart.

quotes.text: many partners in genuine marriages will be kept separate
the statement, which is qualified virtually out of existence

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises the Home Secretary for striking a cynical compromise to appear liberal to the European Court of Human Rights while simultaneously satisfying Conservative backbenchers opposed to allowing husbands of British women to enter.

quotes.text: the Home Secretary will be able to proclaim his liberalism to the European Court of Human Rights
the compact that has been achieved between the Home Secretary and his Back Benchers

top_quote.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that extreme government action against militant suffragists would backfire by encouraging the very movement it aims to suppress, implying sympathy with the suffrage cause.

quotes.text: suggests action which would have one inevitable result only, that of giving a vast encouragement to the movement
the constitutional supporters of Women suffrage are greatly gaining ground

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames the call for crackdown on suffragists as dangerous 'panic legislation,' suggesting measured restraint is preferable to extreme measures against the suffrage movement.

quotes.text: This is essentially an appeal to panic legislation or panic action
the violent section of the women are daily losing public support

top_quote.text: the constitutional supporters of Women suffrage are greatly gaining ground

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues women have rendered effective war service—filling munition factories and replacing men who went to fight—which makes their claim to the franchise reasonable and unanswerable from a practical standpoint.

quotes.text: they say they are perfectly content, if we do not change the qualification of the franchise, to abide by the existing state of things, but that if we are going to bring in a new class of electors
the women of this country have rendered as effective service in the prosecution of the War as any other class of the community

reasons.rationale: The speaker concedes he 'cannot deny' women's claim to be heard on post-war reconstruction questions affecting their interests, framing this as a matter of fairness given their contributions.

quotes.text: have not the women a special claim to be heard on the many questions which will arise directly affecting their interests
I say quite frankly that I cannot deny that claim

reasons.rationale: Despite acknowledging women's claims, the speaker argues that opening the franchise question during wartime would be damaging and divert national effort, so the Government's bill deliberately avoids any modification of franchise qualifications.

quotes.text: nothing more fatal to the concentration of the national effort, than that the floodgates should be opened on all those vast complicated questions of the franchise
the Government have come to the conclusion that they ought to present to Parliament...a Bill that does not in any way modify...the qualification for the franchise

top_quote.text: I say quite frankly that I cannot deny that claim
